text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL CONTRAST
INDICATION: 66-year-old female with biopsy-proven pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head and uncinate process, borderline resectable at diagnosis. Status post 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (modified FOLFIRINOX x6 cycles followed by FOLFIRI x2 cycles secondary to dose-limiting peripheral neuropathy). Prior biliary stenting via ERCP with fully covered metallic stent in place. Restaging evaluation to assess treatment response and candidacy for chemoradiation and/or surgical resection.

COMPARISON: CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol dated at time of mid-treatment restaging (post-cycle 4 FOLFIRINOX) and diagnostic CT abdomen/pelvis at initial presentation.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in three phases following IV administration of iohexol 350 mg/mL: non-contrast, pancreatic parenchymal phase (approximately 40 seconds), and portal venous phase (approximately 70 seconds). Thin-section axial images were reconstructed at 0.625 mm with multiplanar reformations in the coronal and sagittal planes. Oral contrast was not administered. The biliary stent produces minor beam-hardening artifact in the region of the common bile duct and pancreatic head, partially limiting evaluation in that region.

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is a hypoenhancing, ill-defined soft tissue mass centered in the pancreatic head and uncinate process, now measuring approximately 2.6 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 3.0 cm on mid-treatment restaging CT and 3.8 cm at baseline). The mass remains hypoenhancing relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on both the pancreatic parenchymal phase and portal venous phase images. The overall configuration of the lesion has become somewhat less distinct at its margins compared to baseline, a finding that may reflect treatment-related fibrosis and desmoplastic response superimposed on residual viable tumor. No new satellite lesions are identified within the pancreatic parenchyma.

The main pancreatic duct in the body and tail demonstrates persistent but improved upstream dilation, now measuring approximately 5.5 mm in the body (previously 6.5 mm on MRI and approximately 7 mm at initial CT), consistent with partial relief of ductal obstruction. The pancreatic tail parenchyma shows mild volume loss and atrophy compared with the prior study, a finding consistent with chronic obstruction of the main pancreatic duct.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:
Superior mesenteric artery (SMA): The previously described abutment of the pancreatic mass upon the SMA has significantly diminished on the current study. On careful review of the axial and coronal reformatted images, the soft tissue interface between the mass and the SMA encompasses an arc of contact that appears to fall below 90 degrees of the vessel circumference, representing a meaningful improvement from approximately 120 degrees on mid-treatment restaging and approximately 180 degrees at the time of initial presentation. The fat plane between the mass and the SMA, while not fully reconstituted, appears wider than on prior studies, and there is no convincing evidence of frank encasement or luminal narrowing of the SMA. The SMA flows freely without hemodynamically significant stenosis.

Superior mesenteric vein (SMV): The previously noted focal narrowing of the SMV has substantially improved. The SMV is now patent throughout its course without evidence of significant luminal compromise. There is no longer convincing soft tissue cuffing of the SMV. The SMV-portal vein confluence is well opacified and patent. No portal venous thrombosis is identified. The previously described venous narrowing at the level of the uncinate process has resolved on the current examination.

Celiac axis: The celiac axis and its major branches (common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, splenic artery) are patent without evidence of mass encasement or arterial stenosis. No involvement of the gastroduodenal artery stump territory is seen.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
The previously placed fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent is identified within the common bile duct, appearing intact with no evidence of migration. The intrahepatic bile ducts are mildly prominent bilaterally (right greater than left), but notably less dilated than at initial presentation. The common bile duct proximal to the stent shows minimal residual dilation, measuring approximately 5 mm at the hepatic hilum. This is improved compared with the initial common bile duct dilation of 16 mm, reflecting adequate biliary drainage through the indwelling stent.

LIVER:
The liver is mildly enlarged with a smooth contour. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal hypodense or hyperenhancing lesions identified in either the arterial or portal venous phase. No subcapsular lesions are seen. The previously noted mild hepatomegaly at presentation has improved slightly. The hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent.

GALLBLADDER:
The gallbladder is decompressed and not well visualized, consistent with indwelling biliary stenting and underlying biliary obstruction. No cholelithiasis or wall thickening is appreciated on the current study.

SPLEEN:
The spleen is normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions are identified. The splenic vein is patent without evidence of thrombosis or compression.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
The bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits in size and morphology. No adrenal masses or nodules are identified.

KIDNEYS AND RETROPERITONEUM:
The kidneys are symmetric in size and demonstrate normal enhancement bilaterally. No hydronephrosis. No suspicious renal lesions. There is no retroperitoneal soft tissue mass or lymphadenopathy exceeding 1 cm in short axis. Small bilateral perirenal fat-stranding is noted, a nonspecific finding.

LYMPH NODES:
The previously noted peripancreatic lymph nodes have not meaningfully enlarged. The largest peripancreatic node now measures approximately 0.8 cm in short axis (previously up to 0.9 cm at baseline), and borderline nodes in the aortocaval and para-aortic regions remain stable and below threshold for pathologic enlargement. No new regional adenopathy is identified. No mesenteric or retrocrural adenopathy.

PERITONEUM AND ASCITES:
No ascites. No peritoneal thickening, nodularity, or implants to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The omentum is unremarkable. No evidence of serosal implants along the bowel surfaces.

BOWEL:
The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are unremarkable without evidence of wall thickening, obstruction, or pneumatosis. The duodenum demonstrates some mild compression at the C-loop in the region of the pancreatic head mass, unchanged from prior.

CHEST (LUNG BASES):
The lung bases are clear bilaterally without pleural effusion or consolidation. No pulmonary nodules are identified at the lung bases.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
No lytic or blastic osseous lesions are identified within the visualized axial skeleton. The visualized musculature and subcutaneous soft tissues show interval loss of soft tissue and fat volume compared with baseline imaging, consistent with the documented significant weight loss in this patient (approximately 20 pounds from initial presentation through the treatment course). No abdominal wall hernia.

IMPRESSION:

1. PANCREATIC HEAD MASS WITH CONTINUED TREATMENT RESPONSE: The hypoenhancing pancreatic head and uncinate process mass has further decreased in size to approximately 2.6 cm in greatest axial dimension, down from 3.0 cm at mid-treatment restaging and 3.8 cm at diagnosis, representing a cumulative reduction of approximately 32% from baseline following completion of 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The overall appearance is consistent with ongoing treatment response.

2. SIGNIFICANTLY IMPROVED VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS: The degree of soft tissue contact between the primary tumor and the superior mesenteric artery has substantially diminished, now estimated at less than 90 degrees of circumferential contact, a notable interval improvement from the approximately 120-degree abutment at mid-treatment restaging and 180-degree involvement at baseline. The superior mesenteric vein is now patent without evidence of significant narrowing, where previously focal luminal compromise was present. Taken together, these vascular findings suggest meaningful downstaging of the tumor's relationship to critical mesenteric vasculature following neoadjuvant treatment.

3. IMPROVED BILIARY AND PANCREATIC DUCTAL DILATION: Intrahepatic biliary dilation has substantially improved in the setting of the indwelling metallic biliary stent. Upstream main pancreatic duct dilation has also decreased, though mild dilation in the pancreatic body persists.

4. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE: No new hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or pathologically enlarged lymph nodes to suggest locoregional spread or distant metastatic disease.

5. PERIPANCREATIC LYMPH NODES: Previously described peripancreatic lymphadenopathy remains stable and sub-centimeter, with no convincing new nodal disease.

6. INTERVAL SOFT TISSUE VOLUME LOSS consistent with the patient's documented treatment-related weight loss.

Correlation with CA 19-9 level (reported as 85 U/mL at this timepoint, down from 310 U/mL at mid-treatment and 1,204 U/mL at diagnosis) is recommended for comprehensive clinical assessment. The findings on this examination are consistent with continued favorable treatment response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, with improved vascular relationships relative to prior studies. Clinical and multidisciplinary reassessment is warranted to determine candidacy for consolidation chemoradiation and subsequent surgical consideration.
